metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add docs/README.md to WS4 file_impact and extend the verify_command to also check 'grep -q migrations.md docs/README.md' so the docs-index link is verified in CI.
findings.finding: WS4's verify_command checks only CLAUDE.md for the docs/migrations.md link, leaving the 'docs index' half of its own AC unverified and untracked. docs/README.md (the docs index) is absent from WS4's file_impact, so the required link to docs/migrations.md in the docs index has no implementer prompt and no CI check.
findings.location: child 68e1-5dbc-732c-4cd3: WS4 — Acceptance Criteria item 6 + verify_commands
findings.suggested_fix: Add docs/README.md to WS4 file_impact. Change the verify_command to: test -f docs/migrations.md && grep -q marker-gates docs/migrations.md && grep -rq migrations.md CLAUDE.md && grep -q migrations.md docs/README.md

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either update gen_env_registry.py to also enumerate keys from _SECTIONS (so REBAR_ENSURE_* appear automatically), OR remove docs/env-vars.md from WS2's file_impact and add a note that the generator script must be re-run after adding the [ensure] section to _SECTIONS.
findings.finding: The new EnsureConfig env overrides (REBAR_ENSURE_HINT_INTERVAL_SECS, REBAR_ENSURE_HINT_ENABLED) will NOT be auto-detected by gen_env_registry.py because the config system uses a dynamic loop over _SECTIONS in env_overrides() — not a literal os.environ read. No sibling covers updating gen_env_registry.py, creating an owned-by-none gap. Meanwhile WS2's file_impact lists docs/env-vars.md (a 'do not edit by hand' generated file), directly contradicting the parent plan's and WS2's own stated constraint of 'no edits to any generated file.'
findings.location: child 891c-c749-1316-4ab2: WS2 — file_impact + body description of env auto-derivation
findings.suggested_fix: Update gen_env_registry.py to walk _SECTIONS and emit the auto-derived REBAR_<SECTION>_<KEY> names alongside the literal os.environ reads. Add gen_env_registry.py to WS2's file_impact as the file to change (not docs/env-vars.md directly).
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to WS3 AC4 that tracker resolution (config.tracker_dir()) and any StoreError/ConfigError from run_ensures() must both be caught by the best-effort wrapper — the except clause must be broad enough to cover tracker-not-found and config-malformed cases, not just run_ensures() failures.
findings.finding: WS3 wires run_ensures(tracker) at MCP server startup (mcp_server.main) but mcp_server.main() has no tracker-resolution logic — it only does --help check, installs a log handler, then calls build_server().run(). WS3's ACs do not specify how the tracker path is resolved, how an uninitialized store is handled, or whether the entire call is wrapped in a try/except. This is an underspecified dependency that cannot be inferred from WS3's ACs alone.
findings.location: child 424c-c66a-a5c0-49ab: WS3 — AC4 + What section (MCP server startup wiring)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend WS3 AC4 to: 'MCP server startup resolves the tracker via config.tracker_dir(), wraps the entire block (tracker resolution + run_ensures()) in a try/except Exception as e: logger.debug(...), never aborts boot.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Merge the duplicate sentence into a single canonical statement, e.g.: 'Interval + suppression are typed config (`EnsureConfig` in `_config_schema.py`) surfaced as the `[ensure]` config section (`ensure.hint_interval_secs`=86400, `ensure.hint_enabled`=true); env override is auto-derived per rebar's standard mechanism.'
findings.finding: A near-verbatim duplicate sentence in the Design section describes the EnsureConfig / hint config values twice with slightly different framing, creating a near-duplicate requirement that could generate inconsistent implementation if one instance is updated and the other is not.
findings.location: Design section — 'Applied-set marker + write-path pending-hint' bullet, sentence beginning 'Interval + suppression are typed config...' which immediately repeats as 'Interval + suppression live in the `[ensure]` config section...'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the two-sentence fragment with: 'Interval + suppression are typed config (`EnsureConfig` in `_config_schema.py`) surfaced under the `[ensure]` config section (`ensure.hint_interval_secs`=86400, `ensure.hint_enabled`=true); the env override is auto-derived per rebar's standard mechanism — no edits to any generated file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite the garbled/duplicated `EnsureConfig` sentence into a single clear instruction: add `EnsureConfig` dataclass to `_config_schema.py`, register it in `_SECTION_CLASSES` and `_SECTIONS`, and specify the env-override mechanism (or explicitly state none is needed).
findings.finding: The Design section contains a structurally garbled/duplicated sentence about `EnsureConfig` that is an instruction-locality antipattern: the text reads 'EnsureConfig in `_config_schema.py`: `ensure.hint_interval_secs`=86400, `ensure.hint_enabled`=true), Interval + suppression live in the `[ensure]` config section (`ensure.hint_interval_secs`=86400, `ensure.hint_enabled`=true); the env override is auto-derived…' — the same key/value pairs are restated verbatim, and the first parenthetical is left syntactically dangling, making it impossible to determine which sentence carries the authoritative registration instruction.
findings.location: Design section: 'Applied-set marker + write-path pending-hint', second-to-last bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `fsck --repair` behavioral expansion (e.g., a temporary `ensure.enabled` config flag to disable the sweep, or document the exact code revert procedure), so operators have a safe recovery path if the expansion causes unintended side effects.
findings.finding: The plan folds `run_ensures()` into the existing `fsck --repair` command, which can now emit git commits (gitattributes, gitignore) as a side effect when a store was previously misconfigured. While idempotency is argued (second run → zero commits on a healthy store), no explicit rollback/back-out plan is provided for the behavioral expansion of `fsck --repair` itself — e.g., a feature flag to disable ensures in --repair, or an expand-contract sequence that adds the ensures first behind a flag before removing the old calls. Acknowledgement of idempotency does not satisfy T4's rollback/back-out requirement.
findings.location: Design section: 'fsck reconciliation' paragraph; AC line 4 ('ensure-sweep is folded into the EXISTING fsck --repair')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph 'Rollback' section to the plan (and `docs/migrations.md`) stating: (a) the `.ensure-applied`/`.ensure-hinted` files are git-ignored and can be deleted safely, (b) the ensures-in-repair sweep can be disabled via `ensure.hint_enabled=false` (or a new `ensure.repair_enabled` flag), and (c) the old `_migrate_*`/`_ensure_*` hand-listed calls can be restored from git history if needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Design/Concurrency section whether run_ensures() should skip the write lock during fresh-init (since the init-lock already serializes) or acquire both, and document the ordering rationale to prevent a future init-lock → write-lock inversion.
findings.finding: The fresh-init path in init_core (lines 232-255) already holds the mkdir-based init-lock (`ticket-init.lock`) when it executes its ensure-list. The plan directs run_ensures() to acquire the store write lock at ALL init entry points, which would nest the write lock inside the init-lock for the fresh-init arm. The plan provides no analysis of this nested ordering (init-lock → write-lock), leaving the implementer without guidance on whether to skip the write-lock acquisition during fresh-init (safe: the init-lock already serializes fresh-inits) or hold both (adds risk of ordering inversion with any future code that acquires write-lock then init-lock).
findings.location: Design section — 'Entry points (non-hot)' + 'Concurrency'; init.py lines 232-255 (_acquire_init_lock → run_ensures() call ordering)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Concurrency section: 'On the fresh-init arm, the init-lock (mkdir ticket-init.lock) is already held; run_ensures() MAY acquire the write lock inside it (ordering: init-lock → write-lock), which is safe because no existing path acquires the write-lock then the init-lock. Alternatively, skip the write-lock acquisition on fresh-init — since the init-lock serializes the whole fresh-init, no concurrent writer can interfere with the ensure units at this point.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `EnsureConfig` registration to both `_SECTION_CLASSES` (`"ensure": EnsureConfig`) and `_SECTIONS` (`"ensure": {"hint_interval_secs": ..., "hint_enabled": ...}`) in `_config_schema.py`, and call this out explicitly in the plan.
findings.finding: `EnsureConfig` is specified as a new `[ensure]` config section in `_config_schema.py`, but the plan never instructs adding `"ensure": EnsureConfig` to `_SECTION_CLASSES` and `"ensure": {...}` to `_SECTIONS` — the two mandatory registration tables confirmed at `_config_schema.py:407-476`. Without those entries the `coerce_sparse` loop (line 493+) treats `[ensure]` as an unknown section and either warns-and-drops it or hard-errors under `strict=True`, silently ignoring all typed config for the hint subsystem.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `_SECTION_CLASSES`: `"ensure": EnsureConfig` and to `_SECTIONS`: `"ensure": {"hint_interval_secs": lambda v, k: _as_int(v, k, minimum=1), "hint_enabled": lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)}`. Mention this registration step in the Design section alongside the `EnsureConfig` dataclass spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific MCP server startup function/file (e.g. `mcp_server.py:serve()` or equivalent) where `run_ensures` should be called, matching the precision of the init.py line references.
findings.finding: The MCP server startup hook location is unspecified — the plan says 'MCP server startup (best-effort — log-and-continue, never abort boot; under write lock)' but names no specific file, class, or function entry point in the MCP server code. An executor must search the codebase to discover where to insert the `run_ensures` call, which is a stoppable gap.
findings.location: Design section — 'Entry points (non-hot)' bullet; AC line: 'run_ensures() runs unconditionally ... MCP startup (best-effort at startup)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical like '(mcp_server.py: `serve()` or the top-level `main()` before the request loop)' alongside the existing init.py line citations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `set_*` with an explicit list of the specific `set_*` function names to be hooked (e.g. `set_status`, `set_assignee`, ...), or add a note stating the executor should hook all `set_*` that route through `_seam.append_event` and explicitly exclude any that commit inline.
findings.finding: The mutation-path hook list uses `set_*` as a glob rather than enumerating the specific function names, leaving ambiguity about which `set_*` functions are in scope and whether any are on excluded inline-committer paths.
findings.location: Design section — 'Hook the COMMON mutation paths via `_seam.append_event` / `event_append.write_and_push` (create/edit/link/comment/tag/set_*/sign/transition/claim)'
findings.suggested_fix: 'set_status/set_assignee/set_priority (all route via _seam.append_event; any set_* that commits inline is excluded per the compact.py/txn.py rule)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing sub-section to the Design describing the hot-path regression test: what it tests (mutation path calls hint hook but not run_ensures, no subprocess spawn, ≤1 `.ensure-applied` read per process), which test file/module it lives in, and its mechanism (e.g., monkeypatching `run_ensures` to assert non-invocation, mocking `.ensure-applied` reads).
findings.finding: AC10 specifies a 'Hot-path regression test' as a verifiable deliverable, but no testing section or test description exists anywhere in the plan body — it is uncovered work with no design counterpart.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC10: 'Hot-path regression test: mutation ops may hint but never invoke run_ensures (no git-config subprocess; ≤1 marker read/process)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Testing' subsection under Design: 'A regression test in `tests/test_ensures_hotpath.py` patches `run_ensures` to raise if called, then exercises one mutation op (e.g., `create`) and asserts (a) the patch was not triggered and (b) `.ensure-applied` was read at most once (use `unittest.mock.patch` on `open` or a read counter). Also assert no `git config` subprocess is spawned (patch `subprocess.run`/`subprocess.check_output`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the docs/migrations.md scope contradiction: clarify in Context (or a note in AC11) that `docs/migrations.md` is created NOW as a B-tier deliverable covering the ensure pattern, with a section stub for the A-tier ledger (future work) — so both the in-scope B-tier content and the future-work A-tier stub coexist in the same file.
findings.finding: The Context section associates `docs/migrations.md` with A-tier future work ('The A-tier ledger … is future work (docs/migrations.md + doctor idea dabb)'), while AC11 requires `docs/migrations.md` as a current B-tier deliverable documenting the ensure pattern — the same filename serves two contradictory roles.
findings.location: Context section ('docs/migrations.md + doctor idea dabb') vs AC11 ('docs/migrations.md documents reuse, marker-gates-hint-never-repair, hot-path contract, long-lived-MCP trade-off, post-rollback file-ignore, and A-tier future; gc-config is the reference unit')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Context section, change 'is future work (docs/migrations.md + doctor idea dabb)' to 'is future work (a stub section in docs/migrations.md + doctor idea dabb)' to clarify that docs/migrations.md is created in this PR but the A-tier ledger content is a future stub only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the documentation plan to cover user-facing surfaces: add the `[ensure]` config section (both keys, their defaults, and the env-override mechanism) to the project's config reference doc (or equivalent), and add the `ensures: N/M applied` field to the `rebar fsck` CLI help/man page.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `[ensure]` config section with two user-facing typed config options (`ensure.hint_interval_secs`, `ensure.hint_enabled`) and a new `rebar fsck` output field (`ensures: N/M applied`), but the only documentation artifact specified is `docs/migrations.md` — an architectural/developer doc. No update to user-facing configuration reference documentation or CLI help text is planned.
findings.location: Design section: 'Applied-set marker + write-path pending-hint' paragraph; AC line 7 ('typed-config interval (86400s) + [ensure] config-section suppression'); AC line 4 ('plain fsck prints ensures: N/M applied')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md section (or pointer in the existing contributor guide) naming `ensures.py` as the canonical place for all future idempotent init/config steps, explaining the School B decision, the inline-committer carve-out, and the hook-coverage contract so contributors don't bypass the registry.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a first-class architectural convention (all idempotent init/config setup must flow through `ensures.py`) but does not call for a CLAUDE.md update; future contributors adding init steps may bypass the registry, silently re-introducing the exact problem the pattern solves.
findings.suggested_fix: In CLAUDE.md, add a '## Migration / ensure registry' section: 'Any idempotent init-time or config-convergence step MUST be registered in `ensures.py` (not hand-listed in `init.py`). See `docs/migrations.md`. The write-path hint hooks `_seam.append_event` and `event_append.write_and_push`; inline committers (compact.py, txn.py, delete.py) are intentionally excluded — document any new exclusion explicitly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Design section that run_ensures() runs as a DISTINCT phase BEFORE _repair_run() begins (acquiring and releasing the write lock independently), and that _repair_run() itself is never wrapped in the write lock.
findings.finding: The plan says run_ensures() is 'folded into the existing --repair path' under the write lock, but _repair_run() (fsck.py:565-674) does NOT hold the store write lock — it acquires no lock at all and calls `git add`/`git commit` directly inline. The plan does not specify whether run_ensures() (which does acquire the write lock) runs as a strictly prior phase that acquires-then-releases the lock before _repair_run() begins, or is somehow nested inside _repair_run(). An implementer who holds the write lock across both phases would create a scenario where _repair_run()'s git commits contend on the same write lock that is already held.
findings.location: Design section — 'fsck reconciliation' + 'Entry points'; fsck.py:565-674 (_repair_run implementation)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the fsck reconciliation section: 'run_ensures() is called as a distinct phase BEFORE _repair_run() inside the --repair branch of fsck_cli: it acquires the write lock, runs all units, releases it, and returns outcomes. _repair_run() then runs afterward with no write lock (it batch-commits inline). The write lock must NOT be held across both phases.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback schema-state assertion to the Accepted trade-offs section covering: (1) the committed tickets-branch `.gitattributes`/`.gitignore` content is forwards-compatible with old `_commit_gitattributes`/`_commit_gitignore` (idempotent no-op on already-correct content); (2) `gc.auto` unset → old code's `--unset gc.auto` exit 5 is harmless; (3) rollback is code-only and does NOT revert git branch history, so committed ensure-applied changes persist harmlessly.
findings.finding: The plan's post-rollback assertion covers only the git-ignored marker files (.ensure-applied, .ensure-hinted), stating 'old code simply ignores the git-ignored files.' It does NOT assert the schema STATE of the committed tickets-branch content (.gitattributes, .gitignore) or the git-config worktree state (gc.auto, merge.ours driver) after rollback to old code. T11 requires asserting the schema STATE after rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0 or that markers are ignored. The committed branch files are plausibly forwards-compatible (old _commit_gitattributes/_commit_gitignore are idempotent on already-correct content), but the plan fails to affirm this, leaving an unasserted rollback schema state.
findings.location: Accepted trade-offs / post-rollback paragraph; AC (no AC covers post-rollback schema state of committed files or git-config)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Accepted trade-offs' section, extend the post-rollback paragraph: 'After rollback to old code: (1) `.ensure-applied` and `.ensure-hinted` are git-ignored and silently absent to old code — no schema coupling. (2) Committed `.gitattributes`/`.gitignore` on the tickets branch are forwards-compatible: old `_commit_gitattributes` and `_commit_gitignore` are idempotent check-then-act — they no-op when the expected content is already present. (3) `gc.auto` unset: old `_migrate_gc_config` re-runs `--unset gc.auto`; when already absent git exits 5 which is explicitly ignored. No DATA LOSS RISK on rollback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the env-override protocol with the `EnsureConfig` schema spec: either name the explicit `os.getenv("REBAR_ENSURE_...")` read sites (triggering `gen_env_registry.py` scan) or confirm env overrides are NOT needed for this config section.
findings.finding: The plan's claim that 'the env override is auto-derived per rebar's standard mechanism — no edits to any generated file' references a protocol that is never co-located with the `EnsureConfig` schema spec, and the actual mechanism is unresolved: `gen_env_registry.py` (the CI drift-gate confirmed at `scripts/gen_env_registry.py`) only catches literal `os.environ[...]` / `os.getenv(...)` reads — not dataclass field defaults — so `REBAR_ENSURE_HINT_INTERVAL_SECS` only appears in `docs/env-vars.md` if there is an explicit `os.getenv("REBAR_ENSURE_HINT_INTERVAL_SECS", ...)` call somewhere, which the plan never specifies.
findings.location: Design section: 'Applied-set marker + write-path pending-hint' — env override sentence

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit ordering constraint to WS3 AC1: 'run_ensures() is called AFTER _repair_run() returns (data repair first, then config convergence), as a distinct sequential phase within the if do_repair: block.'
findings.finding: WS3's ACs specify that run_ensures() is 'folded into the existing A3 path' but do not constrain that it runs AFTER _repair_run() returns. Running run_ensures() before data repair completes is semantically incorrect (config convergence should follow data repair) and could produce misleading outcomes if a data-repair step creates conditions the ensure units depend on. No AC enforces the post-_repair_run() ordering.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to WS3 AC1: '…run_ensures() is called after _repair_run() completes (not before, not nested inside it), so data repair always precedes config convergence.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
